Available for senior product design roles

One designer. One design language.
Six living products.

I'm a UI/UX designer and frontend developer in Hyderabad. I designed and built the digital ecosystem for Ridhira Group — a luxury wellness company with 34+ spa outlets — covering a native booking app, hospitality and real-estate sites, and a B2B partner platform. Every screen, every design token, and most of the production code is mine.

Case study 01 · Flagship · Shipped screens below are the real product

Ode by Ridhira —
booking a ritual, not a slot.

Lead UI/UX Designer (sole)Mobile · React NativeFigma · ZenotiLive · iOS TestFlight & Google Play
ProblemBooking was fragmented across calls, walk-ins and WhatsApp; members had no digital home; the brand's luxury didn't survive contact with its booking experience.
My roleSole designer — research, visual identity, complete design system, every UI screen, developer handoff.
ConstraintZenoti as the immovable booking and CRM backbone: the design had to make an enterprise API feel like a boutique ritual.
OutcomeLead conversion improved from 58% to 71%; the design system now powers every ongoing release.
THE FIRST FIVE MINUTES · SHIPPED UI · TAP ANY SCREEN TO ZOOM
DECISION 01

Daylight luxury, rationed bronze

Against the wellness-pastel default, the app lives on warm ivory #F5F1EA under a bronze gradient header, with antique gold #B08D57 rationed to primary actions and active states — so the one gold button on any screen is always the next step.

DECISION 02

Booking as a ritual

City → outlet → service → slot → checkout. Each step removes a friction the phone-call baseline forced on guests: distance and open-hours before commitment, a persistent tray while browsing services, and coupon math shown as money saved, not discount noise.

DECISION 03

Membership as a product

A ₹1,00,000 plan can't live in a settings row. Membership gets its own surface — plans, a checkout that restates every benefit beside the total, and a usage dashboard with remaining balance, so value stays visible long after the payment.

01 CITY
→
02 OUTLET
→
03 SERVICE
→
04 SLOT
→
05 CHECKOUT
FIVE SCREENS FROM CITY TO CONFIRMED RITUAL · SCROLL →

The app should feel like the spa — a moment of stillness in the scroll.

Feature · Membership

Selling a ₹1,05,000 decision on a phone screen.

The plan card leads with the benefit that matters — 50% off selected services — then earns the price with validity, a complimentary 90-minute service, and sharing across 8 people.

Checkout restates every benefit beside the GST-inclusive total, so nobody signs a six-figure purchase from memory. And the details screen is honest about its zero state: Inactive, ₹0, 0% used — an empty dashboard that quietly sells the upgrade.

BENEFITS RESTATED AT CHECKOUT · USAGE BAR + REMAINING BALANCE · SHAREABLE AMONG 8
Feature · Gift cards

Gifting that speaks the culture's calendar.

Nine occasions — Holi, Eid, Welcome Baby, Women's Day — each with its own illustrated card, not one generic voucher recoloured. The form pre-fills a warm message and captures the recipient directly, so every gift lands with a name on it.

Checkout renders the exact card the recipient will see — “A little Eidi for your well-being,” from the sender, with value and expiry — turning gifting into an acquisition loop: redeeming means entering the ecosystem.

9 OCCASIONS · PERSONAL MESSAGE DEFAULTED · RECIPIENT CAPTURED AT PURCHASE

The unglamorous screens get the same care.

An empty notifications inbox still keeps its Booking / Membership structure and says something kind instead of nothing. Empty states, guest mode, and OTP fallbacks were designed with the same tokens and the same attention as the checkout — because a luxury product is judged in its quiet moments, not its hero shots.

58% → 71%
LEAD CONVERSION · ZENOTI LEAD-TO-BOOKING FUNNEL · [PERIOD] VS [PERIOD]
What I learned: designing against an enterprise API is a design skill in itself — Zenoti's data model shaped the IA, and knowing what to fight for versus accept shipped the product months earlier. [Add one honest thing that didn't work.]
Case study 02 · Flagship · Live at ridhiraretreats.com — shipped pages below

Ridhira Retreats —
rebuilt around the booking decision.

Lead Product DesignerResponsive webUX audit → phased redesignGA4-measured
ProblemA destination retreat with a website that whispered when it needed to convince — weak conversion, unclear journey, lead forms bleeding inquiries.
My roleFull audit (scored 4.8/10 across the digital experience), end-to-end UX redesign, design system, phased rollout on a live revenue-generating site.
ConstraintShip in phases and measure each — no big-bang relaunch on a site taking real bookings.
OutcomeActive users grew from 11,000 to 15,500 — a 41% increase.
THE SHIPPED HOMEPAGE HERO · A FILM, NOT A PHOTO · TAP TO ZOOM
DECISION 01

A film, not a photo

The hero is a full-bleed, muted film with a single serif promise — “Experience culturally rooted wellness escapes” — and a persistent Book Now. Inspiration first; the navigation waits as a quiet second layer.

DECISION 02

Place is the protagonist

Each retreat leads with its story before its amenities — Casa De Mello opens with c. 1863, a Portuguese legacy, then earns the visit with heritage, wellness and Goan flavours. Editorial serif for the story, quiet sans for the facts.

DECISION 03

The lead form diet

Fields cut, steps split, mobile ergonomics fixed, inline validation added, and a response-time promise made — forms stopped leaking, and Book Now is never more than one gesture away.

Journey · Inspiration → belief

Selling a place by telling its story first.

“Shaped by place, story, and culture” restructured the old brochure into a narrative sequence: a haveli in Rajasthan, a Portuguese home in Goa — each retreat introduced like a character, numbered 01 / 03, with its era, its legacy, and only then its tags.

The section does the trust-building before any ask appears: heritage photography, specifics (“c. 1863”), and a single Explore link per property instead of competing CTAs.

NARRATIVE IA · ERA + LEGACY BEFORE AMENITIES · ONE CTA PER PROPERTY
Journey · Comparison

Experiences you drag through, not scroll past.

The Immersive Experiences rail invites a gesture — drag to explore — through four numbered categories: Heritage & Architecture, Local Flavours, Spa Therapies, Wellness Rituals. Motion becomes the comparison tool.

Cards stay image-first with a single serif label, so the guest compares feelings, not feature lists — and every card leads back toward a retreat page where the booking decision lives.

4 NUMBERED CATEGORIES · GESTURE-DRIVEN · IMAGE-FIRST CARDS

Two design systems, one architect, two correct answers.

11K → 15.5K
ACTIVE USERS (+41%) · GA4 · [PERIOD] VS [PERIOD]
What I learned: auditing before designing changed everything — the 4.8/10 scorecard gave every stakeholder the same map of what was broken, and made the phased roadmap an agreement instead of an argument.
Case study 03 · Flagship

CP Portal — B2B complexity
behind the right doors.

Product DesignerB2B platformRole-based accessDashboards · Payments · Analytics
ProblemChannel partners were running a real-estate business over spreadsheets and phone calls — no shared view of leads, status, or commissions.
My roleUser journeys, dashboard architecture, payment flows, lead analytics, and a role-based access model expressed as UX, not just security.
ConstraintThe same product had to serve three roles coherently — partner, manager, admin — without three separate designs.
OutcomePartner operations moved from spreadsheets to one platform; "where's my commission?" calls answered by a dashboard instead of a phone.
DECISION 01

5 seconds vs 5 minutes

Dashboard hierarchy built on what a partner must see instantly (pipeline, payouts, hot leads) versus what they investigate — density as a designed gradient, not an accident.

DECISION 02

Roles as experiences

One component system, three coherent role views. Permission mapping became an information architecture exercise: what each role sees is what each role needs.

DECISION 03

States are the craft

B2B quality lives in the unglamorous: empty, loading, error, and zero-data states for every table and chart — designed first, not patched later.

3 roles, 1 system
PARTNER · MANAGER · ADMIN — [ADD REAL ADOPTION / OPS METRICS]
What I learned: in B2B, trust is a latency problem — the moment partners could see payout status themselves, the support channel went quiet.
Case study 04 · Systems story

The Ridhira Ecosystem —
four brands, one design language.

Lead UI/UX Designer + Frontend DeveloperNext.js · CMS · Zoho CRMSEO-first4 live sites
ProblemFour brands — corporate, wellness real estate, retreats, property platform — needed shared equity without feeling cloned, and one person had to design and build all of them.
The architectureShared structural tokens (spacing, grid, motion, components) with per-brand expressive tokens (color, type flavor). Ridhira Group sets the quiet-luxury baseline — Cormorant Garamond + DM Sans on warm ivory and champagne; Zen leans cinematic with Framer Motion storytelling; Properties runs lean and fast on Next.js with dynamic inventory.
The plumbingCMS architecture, Zoho CRM lead funnels, SEO-first page structures, and analytics wired into every brand — design and infrastructure shipped as one system.
OutcomeFour live sites from one component library. New brand pages assemble in hours; every brand feels related, none identical.
1 library, 4 brands
SHARED TOKENS · COMPONENTS · CRM · [ADD LIGHTHOUSE / SEO / LEAD METRICS]
What I learned: building the code myself is what made the design system honest — every token had to survive contact with production, so nothing decorative survived.

Deep dives: two of these brands have full standalone case studies — Ridhira Group → · Ridhira Zen →

Also: enterprise healthcare (under NDA). I contributed to a large-scale US government health insurance platform — complex eligibility and enrollment workflows, dashboard experiences, and accessibility-mandated interfaces (Section 508 / WCAG). The work is confidential; the muscle it built is not. Happy to discuss the approach in conversation.

Design systems

Two production systems.
One architecture.

Ode DS runs dark and ritual-slow; Retreats DS runs light and serene. Underneath, they share the same token structure — which is why one designer could keep six products coherent. Click any swatch to copy its hex.

SHARED STRUCTURE: spacing scale · grid · motion rules · component anatomy · accessibility (WCAG AA) — expression diverges, architecture doesn't. This page's own tokens are visible too: toggle { } in the header.

Process

How the work gets made —
and measured.

01 · DISCOVER

Frame the business problem

What does this cost the company today, in money or growth? If nobody can answer, that's the first deliverable.

02 · RESEARCH

Interviews, shadowing, teardown

Guests, front-desk staff, partners; competitor flows; analytics archaeology. I'm hunting for the one insight that redirects the design.

03 · DEFINE

Personas, journeys, IA

The decision sequence users actually follow becomes the information architecture — not the org chart.

04 · SYSTEM FIRST

Tokens before screens

Type scale, color roles, spacing, motion rules. Foundations are why one designer can ship a whole product.

05 · WIREFRAME → PROTOTYPE

Prototype the arguments

Flows tested at low fidelity; disagreements resolved with a clickable prototype instead of a meeting.

06 · VISUAL DESIGN

High fidelity, from the system

Screens assemble from components. Beauty is earned by the thinking, then executed without compromise.

07 · TEST

Usability before launch

Watch real people fail, fix the failure, repeat — cheaper than shipping the failure.

08 · HANDOFF (OR BUILD)

Specs developers use

I've been the developer receiving bad specs. Mine include states, tokens and edge cases — or I just build it myself in React.

09 · MEASURE

Analytics is part of the design file

Every project ends with a number and its provenance — GA4, Zenoti funnels, CRM pipelines. What I refuse to skip: research and measurement.

About

I work the whole stack
of a product decision.

Most designers hand off; most developers receive. I've spent my career refusing that wall. At Ridhira Group I own the path from user research to production deploy — which changes how I design. When you know a component will be built in React with real tokens, you stop drawing decorative one-offs and start designing systems.

How I think

Products earn loyalty through consistency, and consistency is an engineering problem as much as a visual one. So I start every product with foundations — type scale, color roles, spacing, motion rules — before a single screen. The Ode design system is why one designer could ship a full booking product; the shared Ridhira token library is why four brand sites feel like siblings without feeling identical.

How I work with teams

I write specs developers actually use, because I've been the developer receiving bad ones. I prototype arguments instead of debating them. And I treat analytics as part of the design file — every project here ends with a measured outcome, not a Dribbble shot.

Beyond the screen

[Two human sentences: what wellness philosophy taught you about interfaces, what you're exploring in AI-native product design.]
